title: Savoiardi
subtitle: Italian ladyfingers — light sponge fingers for tiramisu and trifle
excerpt: The classic Italian sponge finger: a piped oblong of separated-egg sponge batter dusted with icing sugar before baking, which produces the characteristic lightly crunchy, sugar-crusted exterior over a dry, absorbent crumb. Used in tiramisu, charlottes, and trifles, or eaten alone with coffee.
sourceNotes: Savoiardi, or ladyfingers (boudoir biscuits in the French tradition), are among the oldest documented European sponge biscuits, with origins attributed to the court of Savoy in the fifteenth century. They appear in Italian and French confectionery manuals from the sixteenth century onwards. The method of piping a separated-egg sponge batter and dusting with icing sugar before baking is consistent across all documented versions. Pellegrino Artusi, La scienza in cucina e l'arte di mangiar bene (1891, first edition; widely reprinted), includes a version. The recipe here follows the established formula.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in a single layer, then bag once solid. Use from frozen — they thaw in minutes at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin for up to one week. They dry out and firm further over time, which is useful for tiramisu.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 160°C. Savoiardi bake quickly; the fingers should be pale gold, dry to the touch on top, and feel springy when pressed. They crisp further as they cool.

glossaryTerms.definition: Savoiardi use a separated-egg method: yolks and sugar are beaten until very thick and pale, the whites are beaten separately to stiff peaks, and the two are folded together with flour in alternating additions. The batter should be thick enough to hold its piped shape for 30 seconds without spreading flat. If the whites are underbeaten or the flour is folded too vigorously, the batter slackens and the fingers bake flat. Pipe at a 45-degree angle and keep a steady pressure.

content.text: holding its shape when piped. Beat the yolks until the mixture falls from the whisk in a thick ribbon, beat the whites to firm peaks, and fold with care. The batter loses volume quickly — pipe without delay once it is mixed.

Heat the oven to 180°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper and fit a piping bag with a plain round nozzle of about 1.5 cm diameter. Beat the egg yolks, 80 g of the caster sugar, and the vanilla extract together with an electric mixer for 5 to 6 minutes until very thick, pale, and the mixture falls from the whisk in a ribbon that holds for 3 seconds.

In a clean bowl, whisk the egg whites with the salt until foamy, then add the remaining 40 g of sugar and whisk to firm, glossy peaks. Sift a third of the flour over the yolk mixture and fold in gently, then fold in a third of the whites. Repeat twice more until all the flour and whites are incorporated. Work quickly and do not overfold.

Pipe fingers about 10 cm long onto the lined trays, leaving 3 cm between each. Dust generously with icing sugar through a sieve. Bake for 12 to 14 minutes until pale gold, dry to the touch on top, and springy when lightly pressed. Cool on the trays for 5 minutes (they are fragile while warm) before transferring to a rack.
